setting.note: Luanda on the eve of independence and through the long civil war that follows. A frightened Portuguese woman bricks up her apartment in a grand central building and lives walled in for nearly thirty years, growing food on the terrace while the city churns below. The map holds the old downtown, the bayfront Marginal, the water of the bay and the musseques that ring the city.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. José Eduardo Agualusa, first published in Portuguese as Teoria Geral do Esquecimento, 2012. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: A General Theory of Oblivion, José Eduardo Agualusa (2012). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The rust-red of Luanda's colonial facades and the dust of the musseques. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
